summary: Hotz argues that robotics represents humanity's best chance to escape cloud computing's centralizing, freedom-eroding grip. Unlike web apps where economies of scale favor centralization, robotics has inherent bandwidth and latency requirements that make local compute necessary. He sees this as 'cloud-averse' technology that will naturally resist enshittification. The post connects comma.ai's mission to build 'one perfect box' to a broader vision of individual sovereignty through decentralized robotics, predicting that open source alternatives and on-device learning will keep the robotics ecosystem free from corporate control.
key_points: Technologies can be classified as cloud-favoring (Gmail), cloud-neutral (ChatGPT), or cloud-averse (robotics) based on their bandwidth/latency requirements
Robotics inherently resists cloud centralization because bandwidth and latency requirements are too high for remote compute
Open source alternatives provide 'checks and balances against enshittification' in cloud-averse domains
The future of robotics software is on-device learning with commodity foundation models, not cloud dependence
Physical problems like farming are non-adversarial, making robotics where 'real value in AI comes from'
money_quotes: The cloud is a highway to serfdom.
If the money and users went away, would you leave technology? If you were building this stuff for free for you and your 5 friends, would you keep at it?
Perelman proved the Poincaré conjecture for him and his boys; no businessfuckers showed up and put ads in the proof.
At comma, our goal is to build one perfect box that drives a car better than a human.
Technology itself is upstream of both politics and culture.
The companies will all try to shoehorn cloud into it, but like forcing you to create a cloud account for your microwave, people will see it as stupid.
The way the waves of robotics will break looks bright for individual sovereignty.
We will abolish scarcity; there will be nothing for them to steal they can't have for free.
key_insight: Robotics' inherent need for local compute creates a technological path toward individual sovereignty that politics and culture alone cannot achieve.
